method3.generated_article:
==History==
Prehistoric activity around Jethou is attested by archaeological evidence, including menhirs on the island and late Palaeolithic flint scatters exposed at very low spring tides on the seabed between Crevichon and Jethou.[1][5] The wider Guernsey–Herm–Jethou area formed part of a prehistoric maritime landscape in which the islands were visible from neighbouring coasts and would have been accessible to early seafaring communities.[4] By the late Mesolithic period, rising sea levels had separated Guernsey from the other Channel Islands, leaving earlier land surfaces and occupation evidence submerged around the Bailiwick.[5]

Jethou’s later history is tied to that of the Channel Islands, which have been politically linked with Britain since 1204 while retaining strong Norman and francophone cultural traditions for much of their history.[3] The island is part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, alongside islands such as Herm, Sark, Alderney and Lihou.[3] Modern summaries of Jethou’s past have traced elements of its early identity to Viking or Norse influence, although the island’s documented history is chiefly known through later ownership, tenancy and private occupation.[1]

In the 19th century, Jethou was already notable as a small inhabited or semi-inhabited private island with a main residence, which was built in the early part of that century.[1] The island also entered popular literature through accounts such as *Crusoe Life in the Channel Isles*, which described sailing to Jethou and portrayed its rocky surroundings as hazardous to shipping.[2] Such descriptions emphasized the island’s granite shores, nearby rocks and isolation within the Little Russell between Guernsey and Herm.[2]

In the 20th century, Jethou remained in private occupation under lease from the States of Guernsey, with access generally restricted to residents, workers and invited visitors.[1] The island also had a small philatelic history, with local stamps depicting subjects such as coastal scenery and birds before local stamp issues in the Bailiwick of Guernsey were banned in October 1969.[1] Since 1991, Jethou has been leased to British businessman Sir Peter Ogden, and it remains closed to the public except for occasional limited access arrangements.[1]

==Governance==
Jethou is administered by the government of Guernsey as part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, alongside Guernsey, Lihou and Herm; Alderney and Sark, by contrast, have their own legislatures within the Bailiwick.[5] As part of Guernsey, Jethou falls within a Crown Dependency, meaning it is not part of the United Kingdom or Great Britain, while the British Government is traditionally responsible for defence and foreign affairs and the Crown retains ultimate responsibility for good governance.[1] Jethou is a leased island and is currently not open to the public, unlike Herm, whose lease requires public access between sunrise and sunset.[4] The island’s environmental governance is also linked to Bailiwick-wide conservation arrangements, as Herm, Jethou and The Humps were designated in 2014 as the Bailiwick’s second Ramsar wetland site under Guernsey’s participation in the Ramsar Convention.[2]

==Wildlife==
Jethou forms part of the Herm, Jethou and The Humps Ramsar site, designated on 19 October 2015 as Guernsey’s second wetland of international importance.[4] The site is a shallow marine ecosystem around Herm, Jethou and The Humps, shaped by an unusually large tidal range of up to 10 metres.[1] Guernsey’s position between boreal and Lusitanian marine influences, together with its strong tidal differences, supports a varied range of coastal and marine habitats in the area.[4]  

The Ramsar area supports marine wildlife including Atlantic grey seals, basking sharks, European sea bass and black sea bream, as well as nine recorded bird species including puffins.[1] Its surrounding reefs, seabeds and associated shallow waters have been described as spawning and nursery habitats for fish such as European sea bass and Atlantic mackerel, with eelgrass beds and maerl gravel among the notable marine habitats.[2] The wider site has also been associated with threatened or notable species including green ormer, Balearic shearwater and marine mammals.[2]  

Terrestrial habitat information for Jethou is generally reported together with Herm, and the 2024 State of Nature report for Guernsey and Herm includes Herm, Jethou and The Humps within its assessment area.[3] That report records coastal heathland as a monitored habitat category for Herm and Jethou between 1999 and 2018, reflecting the ecological interest of the smaller islands as well as their surrounding waters.[3]

==Jethou in popular culture==
Jethou has been represented in philately through imagery emphasizing island identity, including a crest with a lion and a fanciful castle-like tower, a map naming the island and its tidal islets, separate designs naming Fauconnière and Crevichon, and an island scene featuring a Neolithic standing stone.[1] Popular prose associated with the island has also highlighted the contrast between its sheltered domestic pleasures, such as sea breezes and garden shade, and the dangers of crossing nearby waters in heavy seas and strong tides.[5]
